lesson: Use parameterized queries or prepared statements for all SQL; string-concatenating user input stays exploitable even behind an escaping function.
Match output encoding to context: HTML-entity-encode inside tag bodies, JS-string-escape inside script blocks, URL-encode in query params — one encoder does not cover every sink.
Pair SameSite=Lax/Strict cookies with a per-request CSRF synchronizer token; SameSite alone fails for cross-subdomain requests and some top-level GET navigations.
Check object-level authorization on every request by verifying the authenticated user can access the specific resource ID requested, not just that a valid session exists — this is what stops IDOR.
Call exec functions with an argv array instead of shell=True plus string interpolation; passing argv bypasses shell parsing entirely and neutralizes command-injection metacharacters.
Replace static long-lived API keys with short-lived tokens minted via workload-identity or instance-metadata services, so a leaked credential expires in minutes instead of indefinitely.
For template engines, never pass user input as the template source itself; bind it only as data into a fixed template, since sandboxed engines still leak via attribute/method access chains (SSTI).
Grant standing admin roles rarely; instead use time-boxed just-in-time privilege elevation that auto-revokes after minutes, so a compromised account has a narrow blast-radius window.
Deploy a nonce-based Content-Security-Policy with strict-dynamic instead of a host allowlist, since allowlisted domains are routinely bypassed via their own JSONP or reflected-script endpoints.
